text:

Use the sidebar to navigate unit packs, teaching notes, and supporting quiz materials for each chapter.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 15 — Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
Learning objectives
Construct and interpret scatter plots; describe direction, form, and strength; calculate and interpret Pearson r; explain limitations of correlation; distinguish correlation from causation.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 15 — Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 15 — Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A marketing director sees r = 0.72 between advertising spend and sales and says ‘advertising explains 72% of our sales variation.’ What is wrong with this statement, and how should r be interpreted correctly?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): Two stores have the same correlation between ad spend and sales. One is a city-centre store; the other is a rural superstore. Should the company use the same advertising strategy for both? What does the correlation tell you, and what does it not tell you?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A dataset of 50 countries shows a strong positive correlation between the number of mobile phones per person and life expectancy. What confounding variable is almost certainly responsible, and why would it be wrong to conclude that mobile phones increase life expectancy?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 15 — Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 15 — Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often say ‘r = 0.62 means advertising explains 62% of the variation in sales.’ This confuses r with R-squared. r = 0.62 means R-squared = 0.38, which means 38% of variation is explained. Drill this distinction explicitly.
Misconception 2: Students assume r = 0 means no relationship. Correct this: r = 0 means no LINEAR relationship. A perfect U-shaped curve would produce r near 0. Always look at the scatter plot first.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 15 — Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 15 — Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
Dataset notes
ch15_main.csv: 120 retail stores. Use ad_spend_gbp and weekly_sales_gbp as the primary pair; weekly_footfall as a mediating variable; promotion_active and store_format as grouping variables.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 09 — Quartiles, Boxplots, IQR, Outliers, and Skewness
text:
Learning objectives
Calculate quartiles and IQR; construct and read boxplots; apply the IQR fence rule; describe skewness and relate it to mean/median relationship; select appropriate summaries for skewed data.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 09 — Quartiles, Boxplots, IQR, Outliers, and Skewness

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 09 — Quartiles, Boxplots, IQR, Outliers, and Skewness
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A dataset of annual executive compensation has a median of GBP 180,000 and a mean of GBP 340,000. What does this tell you about the distribution? Would you report the median or mean to describe a typical executive’s pay?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A quality analyst flags 22 product measurements as outliers using the IQR rule. Her manager says: ‘Delete them all.’ What would you advise, and what process would you follow instead?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): Two boxplots are shown for delivery times: one for urban customers, one for rural. The rural box is taller and the median line is closer to Q3. Describe what each of these features indicates.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 09 — Quartiles, Boxplots, IQR, Outliers, and Skewness

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 09 — Quartiles, Boxplots, IQR, Outliers, and Skewness
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often treat the IQR fence rule as a deletion rule. Clarify that it is a flagging rule. Outliers require investigation. Some are genuine; some are errors; some require specialist knowledge to judge.
Misconception 2: Students sometimes believe skewness can only be detected from a histogram. Show them that mean > median implies right skew and demonstrate this with the property price data.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 09 — Quartiles, Boxplots, IQR, Outliers, and Skewness

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 09 — Quartiles, Boxplots, IQR, Outliers, and Skewness
text:
Dataset notes
ch09_main.csv: 180 property listings. Use asking_price_gbp for full quartile and outlier analysis; property_type for grouped boxplots; days_on_market for skewness discussion.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 20 — Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
Learning objectives
State null and alternative hypotheses; explain the logic of hypothesis testing; distinguish Type I and Type II errors; explain the trade-off between error rates; describe business consequences of each error type.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 20 — Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 20 — Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A pharmaceutical company tests a new drug at alpha = 0.05. If the drug genuinely works, what is the probability of a Type II error if power is 0.70? What does this mean for patients who might benefit?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A retailer uses alpha = 0.01 for all A/B tests. A marketer complains that too many promising campaigns are being rejected. Is this complaint consistent with Type I or Type II error concerns? What adjustment would you suggest?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A company runs 20 hypothesis tests simultaneously, each at alpha = 0.05. Even if all null hypotheses are true, how many false positives would you expect? What does this imply for how results should be reported?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 20 — Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 20 — Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often think ‘failing to reject H0’ means H0 is proved true. Correct this with the absence-of-evidence framing: failing to reject H0 means the data are consistent with it, not that it has been confirmed.
Misconception 2: Students often set alpha after seeing the data, choosing a level that makes the result significant. Stress that alpha must be set before data collection. Changing alpha post-hoc inflates the true Type I error rate.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 20 — Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 20 — Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
Dataset notes
ch20_main.csv: 160 employees (80 treatment, 80 control). Use group and weekly_tasks for hypothesis framing; weekly_errors and satisfaction for secondary outcomes.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 11 — Probability Basics
text:
Learning objectives
Define probability; distinguish classical, empirical, and subjective approaches; apply complement and addition rules; calculate conditional probability; assess independence.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 11 — Probability Basics
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A manager reviews complaint data and says ‘We get about 4 complaints per week, so next week we’ll get 4.’ What is wrong with this statement, and how does probability thinking improve it?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): Using the incident dataset, the resolution rate for theft reports is lower than for refund requests. Does this mean theft reports are harder to resolve, or could something else explain the difference?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): Explain why P(A|B) and P(B|A) are not the same thing. Give a business example where confusing them would lead to a poor decision.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 11 — Probability Basics
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often think conditional probability means restricting to a subset of the dataset. That is operationally correct but conceptually it should be framed as: ‘In the world where B has occurred, what is the probability of A?’
Misconception 2: Students confuse mutually exclusive events with independent events. Clarify: mutually exclusive means they cannot both occur; independent means one’s occurrence does not affect the other’s probability. Two events can be mutually exclusive but not independent.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 11 — Probability Basics
text:
Dataset notes
ch11_main.csv: 200 retail incidents. Use incident_type for empirical probability; resolved_at_counter for conditional probability; day_of_week for independence testing.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 14 — Binomial and Poisson Distributions
text:
Learning objectives
Identify binomial conditions; calculate binomial probabilities using formula and R; identify Poisson conditions; calculate Poisson probabilities using formula and R; distinguish the two distributions.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 14 — Binomial and Poisson Distributions

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 14 — Binomial and Poisson Distributions
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A call centre manager says: ‘We receive 300 calls per hour on average, so we need exactly 300 call handlers per hour.’ What is wrong with this reasoning, and how does the Poisson distribution improve it?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A quality inspector checks 10 items. The defect rate is 8%. Using the binomial distribution, what is the probability of finding no defective items? What is the probability of finding more than 2?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): Explain why the binomial distribution is not appropriate for modelling the number of road accidents per month on a busy motorway. Which distribution would be more appropriate, and why?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 14 — Binomial and Poisson Distributions

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 14 — Binomial and Poisson Distributions
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often apply the binomial when the independence condition is violated, for instance when sampling without replacement from a small population. For small populations, the hypergeometric distribution is more appropriate, but the binomial is a good approximation when the population is at least 20 times the sample size.
Misconception 2: Students confuse the Poisson mean lambda with a rate per trial. Emphasise that lambda is always defined relative to a specific interval: arrivals per hour, defects per 100 metres, calls per day. The interval must be stated.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 14 — Binomial and Poisson Distributions

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 14 — Binomial and Poisson Distributions
text:
Dataset notes
ch14_main.csv: 300 A&E records. Use admitted for binomial probability; hour_of_arrival counts for Poisson; triage_category for conditional admission probabilities.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 04, Sampling Methods
text:
Learning objectives
Describe and distinguish four probability sampling methods; explain when each is most appropriate; identify the weaknesses of convenience and purposive sampling; evaluate a sampling design for a given research question.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 04, Sampling Methods
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): You are designing a survey of 200 employees across a firm with offices in five cities. Which sampling method would you recommend? Justify your choice.
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A research team selects participants by posting a link on social media. What type of sampling is this? What biases might result? Can the findings be generalised to the population?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): Stratified sampling requires knowing the strata in advance. Give two situations in which this prior knowledge might be unavailable or unreliable, and explain what you would do.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 04, Sampling Methods
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often assume any large sample is representative. Correct this: a biased sampling frame produces biased results regardless of sample size. The 1936 Literary Digest poll is a classic illustration.
Misconception 2: Students sometimes believe random sampling means arbitrary or haphazard selection. Clarify: random sampling is a deliberate, probability-based procedure in which every member has a known, non-zero probability of selection.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 04, Sampling Methods
text:
Dataset notes
ch04_main.csv: 600 employees across three departments. Simulate and compare SRS, systematic, stratified, and convenience samples.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 19 — Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
Learning objectives
Construct a CI for a population proportion; interpret it correctly; calculate required sample size for a given margin of error; compare CIs across subgroups; explain the conservative estimate.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 19 — Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 19 — Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A polling company reports: ‘Support for the proposal is 54%, margin of error ±3 percentage points.’ What does this mean? Can we conclude the proposal has majority support?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A company wants to survey customers with a ±2% margin of error at 95% confidence. They have prior data suggesting satisfaction is around 70%. How many respondents are needed? How does this compare with the conservative estimate?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): Two channels both show p-hat = 0.72 satisfied, but Channel A has n = 40 and Channel B has n = 400. Compare the confidence intervals for both. What does this tell you about drawing conclusions from small subgroups?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 19 — Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 19 — Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often use z* = 1.96 for proportions and forget to check the validity conditions (np >= 10 and n(1-p) >= 10). Walk through a case where these conditions fail and explain why the normal approximation breaks down.
Misconception 2: Students confuse the margin of error (half the CI width) with the full CI width. Clarify: a margin of error of ±3% produces an interval of 6 percentage points wide.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 19 — Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 19 — Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
Dataset notes
ch19_main.csv: 500 satisfaction survey responses. Use satisfied for the primary proportion; channel and age_group for subgroup CIs; overall p-hat for sample size planning demonstration.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 07 — Graphs for Categorical and Quantitative Data
text:
Learning objectives
Select appropriate chart types for given variable types and questions; construct bar charts, histograms, scatter plots, and line graphs; identify misleading graphs; apply design principles.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 07 — Graphs for Categorical and Quantitative Data

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 07 — Graphs for Categorical and Quantitative Data
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): Your team is presenting quarterly sales by region to the board. Which chart type would you choose, and why? What would you avoid?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A colleague shows a bar chart where the y-axis starts at 95. Sales appear to have tripled. The actual values are 97 and 103. What should you do before presenting this chart?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A scatter plot shows a positive association between marketing spend and revenue. A manager concludes that increasing marketing spend causes revenue to rise. What is right and what is wrong about this interpretation?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 07 — Graphs for Categorical and Quantitative Data

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 07 — Graphs for Categorical and Quantitative Data
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often use pie charts for more than 5 categories. Demonstrate that with 8 categories, a pie chart is unreadable. Show the same data as an ordered bar chart.
Misconception 2: Students treat histograms and bar charts as interchangeable. Correct this with the key distinction: histograms require adjacent bars and are for continuous data; bar charts have gaps and are for categories.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 24 — One-Way ANOVA
text:
Learning objectives
Explain why multiple t-tests inflate Type I error; state ANOVA hypotheses; interpret ANOVA table; conduct Tukey post-hoc; state assumptions.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 24 — One-Way ANOVA
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1: ANOVA gives F = 18.4, p < 0.001. A manager says ’‘’Provider Gamma is the best.’’’ What does the ANOVA result actually tell you, and what additional analysis is needed?
DQ2: Four providers, six pairs. If each t-test uses alpha = 0.05, what is the probability of at least one false positive? Calculate this and explain the implication.
DQ3 (challenge): Levene’’’s test gives p = 0.03, suggesting unequal variances. Should standard ANOVA still be used? What is the alternative?

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 24 — One-Way ANOVA
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often misread the ANOVA table: they report the F statistic’‘’s p-value as if it answers’‘’which pairs differ.’’’ It does not. Always follow a significant ANOVA with post-hoc tests.
Misconception 2: Students confuse eta-squared with r-squared from regression. Both measure proportion of variance explained, but eta-squared applies to categorical predictors and is not the square of a correlation.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 17 — Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Learning objectives
Explain sampling distributions; state the CLT and its conditions; calculate and interpret standard error; explain the square root law; describe why CLT makes normal-based inference broadly applicable.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 17 — Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 17 — Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A quality manager says: ‘I take a sample of 30 items every hour and compute the mean weight. My process is stable.’ What is the distribution of those hourly sample means, and what determines how spread out they are?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): The population of fulfilment hours is right-skewed. Looking at Figure 17.2, at what sample size do the sampling distributions look approximately normal? What would change if the population were even more skewed?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A colleague says: ‘The CLT means that if I take a large enough sample, my data will be normally distributed.’ Correct this misstatement precisely.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 17 — Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 17 — Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often confuse the standard deviation of the sample (s) with the standard error of the mean (s/sqrt(n)). Drill this distinction: SD describes spread in the data; SE describes precision of the mean estimate.
Misconception 2: Students think the CLT says the population becomes normal with large n. Correct this: the CLT says the SAMPLING DISTRIBUTION of the mean becomes normal. The population shape does not change.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 17 — Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

text:
Learning objectives
Distinguish goodness-of-fit from independence tests; construct contingency tables; compute chi-square; interpret Cramér’’’s V; state assumptions.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 25 — Chi-Square Tests
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1: The chi-square test is significant. A product manager says ’‘’budget customers don’‘’t buy online.’’’ Is this what the test shows? What does it actually tell you?
DQ2: One cell has an expected frequency of 3. What problem does this create? What would you do?
DQ3 (challenge): A dataset has 5,000 customers. Even a trivial association between segment and channel produces a highly significant chi-square. How should Cramér’’’s V be used to assess the finding?

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 25 — Chi-Square Tests
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students compute chi-square on percentages rather than counts. Correct this: chi-square requires raw frequencies, not proportions, because the formula uses the grand total n.
Misconception 2: Students think a significant chi-square test tells them which cells are driving the association. It does not. Adjusted residuals (or separate tests) are needed for cell-level interpretation.

text:
Discussion questions

DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): Two delivery companies have the same expected delivery time of 3.2 days. Company A has a standard deviation of 0.4 days; Company B has 2.1 days. For a retailer who has promised next-day delivery, which company is preferable and why?

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 13 — Random Variables, Expected Value, and Probability Distributions
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often think the expected value must be an achievable value. Use the classic example: expected children per family = 1.9. This is not an achievable value; it is a long-run average.
Misconception 2: Students confuse a frequency distribution (from observed data) with a probability distribution (a model). A frequency distribution describes what happened; a probability distribution describes what could happen and with what probability.

text:
Learning objectives

crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 23 — Independent and Dependent Samples t-Tests

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 23 — Independent and Dependent Samples t-Tests
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1: A manager wants to know whether Team A’’’s higher post-training score is due to the training or whether Team A was always better. How would the study design need to change to answer this?
DQ2: Why does the paired t-test give a larger t statistic than the unpaired test on the same data? Explain without mathematics.
DQ3 (challenge): A researcher measures 20 employees’’’ productivity on Monday and Friday. She uses an independent t-test. What error has she made?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 23 — Independent and Dependent Samples t-Tests

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 23 — Independent and Dependent Samples t-Tests
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often apply independent t-test to paired data because they see ‘two groups’. The diagnostic question is: does each observation in group 1 correspond to a specific observation in group 2?
Misconception 2: Students think var.equal=TRUE and var.equal=FALSE always give similar results. Show a case with very different group SDs where the conclusion differs.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 08 — Mean, Median, Mode, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
Learning objectives
Calculate and interpret mean, median, and mode; explain when each is appropriate; calculate variance and standard deviation; interpret SD in context; use measures together to describe a distribution.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 08 — Mean, Median, Mode, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 08 — Mean, Median, Mode, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A supermarket reports that the mean weekly spend of its loyalty card holders is GBP 87. A second report says the median is GBP 54. What does this difference tell you about the distribution of spending? Which figure is more useful for planning stock levels?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): Two investment portfolios have the same mean annual return of 8%. Portfolio A has a standard deviation of 2%; Portfolio B has a standard deviation of 14%. How should a risk-averse investor interpret this information?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A colleague calculates the standard deviation using n rather than n-1 in the denominator and gets a slightly smaller result. Does it matter? When would the difference be meaningful?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 08 — Mean, Median, Mode, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 08 — Mean, Median, Mode, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often cannot interpret standard deviation directly. Give them a concrete example: call duration mean 4.2 mins, SD 1.8 mins. Ask what a SD of 1.8 means in practical terms for staffing.
Misconception 2: Students confuse variance (squared units, hard to interpret) with standard deviation (original units). Always show both and explain why SD is the reported measure in practice.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output
text:
Learning objectives
Select the correct test for any standard business question; translate a business question into formal hypotheses; read and report complete test output; recognise and correct common reporting errors; apply the six-step analytical workflow.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A colleague has run a one-way ANOVA on five departments and found p = 0.03. She says: “Operations is the best-performing department.” What has she missed, and what should she do next?
DQ2 (groups, 10 min): Each group receives one of the following scenarios and must: (a) select the correct test; (b) state H0 and H1; (c) identify the key output elements to report. - Scenario A: Does customer satisfaction differ between the North and South regions? - Scenario B: Is there an association between product category and whether a complaint was escalated? - Scenario C: Did the office refurbishment improve employee wellbeing scores? - Scenario D: Do the three contract types differ in average contract value?
DQ3 (individual reflection): Think of one decision you (or an organisation you know) made based on data in the past year. Which test from this course would have been appropriate? What was the risk if no formal analysis was done?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students think the hardest part of statistics is the calculation. Correct this: the calculations are handled by R. The hard part — and the high-value skill — is asking a precise question, selecting the right tool, and communicating the answer clearly.
Misconception 2: Students treat “p < 0.05, significant” as a complete conclusion. Show Table 27.4 and ask students to count how many additional elements are missing from that report. The complete report is never optional.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output
text:
Capstone group exercise
Divide students into groups of 3-4. Each group must: 1. Choose two new questions answerable from ch27_main.csv (different from the five in the chapter). 2. Complete the six-step workflow for each. 3. Present a 2-minute verbal report to the class.
Suggested additional questions available in the dataset: - Does mean engagement differ across the four regions? - Is turnover intention associated with department? - Do employees with longer tenure earn significantly more than the benchmark? - Does salary differ between trained and untrained employees?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output
text:
Dataset notes
ch27_main.csv: 300 employees. Variables span all major test types: - Continuous: productivity, salary_gbp, tenure_years - Ordinal: engagement_score (1-7) - Binary: training_completed, turnover_intention - Nominal: department, region, satisfaction_band
This dataset is specifically designed to support all test types from a single source, demonstrating that real organisational data supports multiple simultaneous analyses.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 27 — Choosing the Right Test and Reading Output
text:
Assessment
Moodle quiz: quiz27.xml — includes scenario-based test selection and output interpretation questions.
Final assessment recommendation: a short (500-word) analytical memo in which the student selects and runs two tests on a dataset of their choice, reports the results completely, and writes a one-paragraph recommendation for a non-technical manager.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 18 — Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
Learning objectives
Explain what a confidence interval represents; construct a CI for a mean using the t-distribution; interpret a CI correctly; explain the effect of confidence level and sample size on width; compare CIs across subgroups.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 18 — Confidence Intervals for Means

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 18 — Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A report states: ‘Average delivery time is 28 minutes (95% CI: 26.1 to 29.9).’ A manager asks: ‘Does this mean 95% of deliveries take between 26 and 30 minutes?’ How would you respond?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A researcher increases her sample size from 50 to 200. By roughly what factor does the width of her 95% CI change? Show the calculation.
DQ3 (challenge, optional): Two studies on the same topic report: Study A: mean = 45, 95% CI [42, 48]; Study B: mean = 47, 95% CI [41, 53]. Which study provides stronger evidence, and why?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 18 — Confidence Intervals for Means

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 18 — Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often say a 95% CI means there is a 95% probability the true mean is inside it. Correct this: the 95% refers to the long-run coverage probability of the PROCEDURE, not to a probability about a fixed but unknown true mean.
Misconception 2: Students assume a wider CI is always worse than a narrower one. Clarify: a wider CI at a higher confidence level may be more honest about genuine uncertainty than a narrow CI at a low confidence level.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 18 — Confidence Intervals for Means

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 18 — Confidence Intervals for Means
text:

ch18_main.csv: 240 food delivery records. Use delivery_minutes for the primary CI; weekend and rainy as subgroup variables; cuisine_type for multiple group comparison.

crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 22 — Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 22 — Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1: A factory reports that mean fill is 499.4 ml against a 500 ml target. A quality manager says ‘the difference is only 0.6 ml, so it’‘’s fine.’ At what scale of production does 0.6 ml become significant?
DQ2: The t-test gives p = 0.04. A colleague says: ‘We can be 96% sure the machine is underfilling.’ Correct this interpretation precisely.
DQ3 (challenge): If alpha were set to 0.01 instead of 0.05, would the conclusion change? What does this illustrate about the choice of alpha?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 22 — Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 22 — Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often report only ‘p < 0.05, significant’ and stop. Insist on a complete report: t, df, p, CI, Cohen’’’s d, and a plain-language conclusion.
Misconception 2: Students assume z-test and t-test always give the same answer. Show that with small n, the t critical value is substantially larger than 1.96, making the t-test more conservative.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 21 — One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
Learning objectives
Distinguish one-tailed and two-tailed tests; interpret p-values correctly; explain the alpha-p relationship; calculate effect size; distinguish statistical from practical significance; describe what power means.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 21 — One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 21 — One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): An e-commerce analyst reports: ‘Version B has a higher conversion rate, p = 0.03.’ The manager says: ‘Great, so there is only a 3% chance that version A is better.’ Correct this interpretation precisely.
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A study with 5,000 participants finds that people who drink one extra cup of coffee per day have blood pressure that is 0.2 mmHg higher (p = 0.001). Is this finding practically significant? What would you advise a health-conscious manager?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A test is run with 80% power. The result is not significant (p = 0.12). Should the team conclude the intervention does not work? What alternative explanations exist, and what would you recommend next?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 21 — One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 21 — One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students treat p = 0.049 and p = 0.051 as categorically different. Correct this: the alpha threshold is a convention, not a sharp boundary. Both results convey similar levels of evidence and both warrant thoughtful interpretation.
Misconception 2: Students confuse effect size with the difference between means. Cohen’s d is standardised by the pooled SD, making it comparable across studies. A raw difference of 5 points means nothing without knowing the SD.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 21 — One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 10 — The Normal Curve, Empirical Rule, and Z-Scores
text:
Learning objectives
Describe normal distribution properties; apply the empirical rule; calculate and interpret z-scores; compare performance across distributions using z-scores; assess normality visually.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 10 — The Normal Curve, Empirical Rule, and Z-Scores

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 10 — The Normal Curve, Empirical Rule, and Z-Scores
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A company produces components with a mean diameter of 50 mm and SD of 0.4 mm. Using the empirical rule, between what two values would you expect 95% of components to fall? What proportion would you expect to fall outside this range?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A student scored 68 in statistics (class mean 60, SD 10) and 75 in marketing (class mean 72, SD 4). In which subject did they perform better relative to their class? Show your working.
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A colleague claims: ‘Our data is normally distributed, so we can ignore extreme values.’ Is this correct? What does the normal distribution actually say about values beyond 3 standard deviations?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 10 — The Normal Curve, Empirical Rule, and Z-Scores

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 10 — The Normal Curve, Empirical Rule, and Z-Scores
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often apply the empirical rule to non-normal data. Stress that the rule applies only when the distribution is approximately normal. Show a right-skewed example where the rule clearly fails.
Misconception 2: Students confuse standardisation (computing z-scores) with normalisation (scaling to 0-1). These are different operations. Z-scores can be negative and have no fixed upper bound.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 10 — The Normal Curve, Empirical Rule, and Z-Scores

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 26 — Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Learning objectives
Explain when non-parametric tests are preferred; conduct Mann-Whitney, Wilcoxon, Kruskal-Wallis, and Friedman tests; select appropriately; report effect size.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 26 — Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1: A researcher collects 7-point Likert satisfaction scores from two departments (n = 15 each). Should she use an independent t-test or Mann-Whitney U? What factors affect this decision?
DQ2: The Kruskal-Wallis test is significant. What is the next step, and why is it needed?
DQ3 (challenge): A colleague says: ’‘’Non-parametric tests are always safer because they make no assumptions.’’’ Is this correct? What do non-parametric tests assume?

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 26 — Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students think non-parametric tests are completely assumption-free. Correct this: they assume independence of observations and, for tests of location difference, that the distributions have similar shape.
Misconception 2: Students believe non-parametric tests are always less powerful. Correct this: when parametric assumptions are violated, non-parametric tests can be more powerful than their nominal parametric equivalents.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 12 — Addition Rule, Multiplication Rule, Permutations, and Combinations
text:
Learning objectives
Apply the general addition rule; apply the multiplication rule for independent and dependent events; construct probability trees; calculate permutations and combinations; use counting rules for probability.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 12 — Addition Rule, Multiplication Rule, Permutations, and Combinations

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 12 — Addition Rule, Multiplication Rule, Permutations, and Combinations
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A supplier has two factories. Factory A produces 60% of the company’s output and has a 2% defect rate. Factory B produces 40% and has a 5% defect rate. If you receive a defective item, which factory is it more likely from? (This previews Bayes’ theorem.)
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): A project team of 5 needs to assign a lead, a deputy, and a recorder. How many ways can this be done? Would the answer change if the three roles were identical?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): Two quality inspectors check the same batch independently. Inspector A has an 80% detection rate; Inspector B has a 75% detection rate. What is the probability that at least one detects a defect if one is present?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 12 — Addition Rule, Multiplication Rule, Permutations, and Combinations

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 12 — Addition Rule, Multiplication Rule, Permutations, and Combinations
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often apply the simple addition rule P(A) + P(B) when events overlap. Use a Venn diagram to show concretely why the intersection must be subtracted.
Misconception 2: Students confuse combinations and permutations. The key question is always: does the order of selection matter for the purpose at hand? Assign prizes = order matters; form a committee = order does not.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 12 — Addition Rule, Multiplication Rule, Permutations, and Combinations

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 12 — Addition Rule, Multiplication Rule, Permutations, and Combinations
text:
Dataset notes
ch12_main.csv: 250 quality batches. Use shift and supervisor_present for the general addition rule; defect rates for multiplication rule; machine selection for counting examples.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 12 — Addition Rule, Multiplication Rule, Permutations, and Combinations

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 16 — Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
Learning objectives
Fit and interpret a simple linear regression; use the equation for prediction; interpret R-squared; examine residuals; explain why correlation does not imply causation; identify confounding variables.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 16 — Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 16 — Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A regression shows that each additional year of tenure is associated with GBP 800 higher salary. HR proposes keeping employees longer to reduce salary costs. What logical error is being made?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): The residual plot for a regression of productivity on training hours shows a fan shape: residuals are small for low fitted values and large for high ones. What problem does this indicate, and how might you address it?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A study of 500 schools finds that schools with more computers per student have higher exam pass rates (r = 0.58). A government official concludes that giving every school more computers will raise pass rates. Evaluate this conclusion using the three conditions for causation.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 16 — Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 16 — Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often think a high R-squared validates a causal claim. Correct this explicitly: R-squared measures fit, not causation. A regression of shoe size on reading ability in children would produce a high R-squared because both grow with age, but neither causes the other.
Misconception 2: Students struggle with the intercept when X = 0 is outside the observed range. In the training-productivity model, b0 is the predicted productivity for zero training hours — a value that may not be meaningful or observed. Always check whether the intercept is interpretable before reporting it.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 16 — Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 16 — Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
Dataset notes
ch16_main.csv: 200 employees. Use training_hours to predict productivity_score; tenure_years as a confounder; salary_gbp for multiple regression extension.

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 01, What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
Learning objectives
Define statistics; distinguish descriptive from inferential; explain the role of statistical thinking in management; identify cognitive biases; describe the five-step cycle.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 01, What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 01, What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): Think of a decision made recently at your workplace or by an organisation in the news. What data were used, if any? Was the reasoning descriptive, inferential, or absent?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): Which cognitive bias, availability, confirmation, or small-sample thinking, do you think is most dangerous in a business setting, and why?
DQ3 (challenge, optional): A report states that customer satisfaction rose from 3.6 to 3.9 after a new training programme. What questions would you ask before concluding the programme worked?
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 01, What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 01, What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students often believe that a large dataset automatically produces reliable conclusions. Correct this: large datasets can contain systematic bias; size does not fix poor data collection.
Misconception 2: Many students equate statistical significance with practical importance. Flag this early: significance depends on sample size; even a trivial difference can be significant in a large sample.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 01, What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 05, Variable Types and Measurement Scales
text:
Learning objectives
Classify variables as categorical or numerical; distinguish nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio scales; select appropriate summary statistics for each type; identify consequences of applying the wrong summary to a given variable type.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 05, Variable Types and Measurement Scales

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 05, Variable Types and Measurement Scales
text:
Discussion questions
DQ1 (whole class, 5 min): A hotel chain rates customer satisfaction on a scale of 1 to 10. A manager computes the mean and reports it to two decimal places. Is this appropriate? What would you recommend instead?
DQ2 (small groups, 7 min): Identify three variables from your own industry or area of study. For each, state its measurement level and the most appropriate summary statistic.
DQ3 (challenge, optional): Why does the distinction between interval and ratio scales matter? Give an example in a business context where using an interval scale as if it were a ratio scale would produce a misleading conclusion.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 05, Variable Types and Measurement Scales

title: Instructor Pack: Unit 05, Variable Types and Measurement Scales
text:
Common misconceptions
Misconception 1: Students frequently compute means for ordinal variables, particularly Likert-scale items. Acknowledge the real-world ambiguity: many researchers treat 5- or 7-point scales as interval for practical purposes, but explain why this is debated and what the safer approach is.
Misconception 2: Students confuse discrete and continuous variables with categorical and numerical. Clarify: discrete and continuous describe sub-types of numerical variables; they are not the same as the nominal/ordinal/interval/ratio hierarchy.
crumbs: Instructor Pack: Unit 05, Variable Types and Measurement Scales
